title: 10 Tahsas — St. Matra & The Assembly
narrative:
The tenth day of Tahsas commemorates Saint Matra, an early martyr venerated in the Ethiopian and Coptic traditions, whose name has been interpreted within the Ge'ez lexical tradition as bearing connotations of rain or dew — those gentle gifts from heaven that cause the parched earth to bring forth its fruit, and that the prophets of Israel employed as images of the divine grace descending upon the soul. The details of Matra's martyrdom preserved in the synaxarial tradition speak of a witness who endured suffering with steadfast serenity, offering his or her death as a libation upon the altar of divine love, and thereby joining the great company of those who by their blood have watered the growth of the Church.

Saint Menas the Wonder-Worker, one of the most celebrated martyrs of the Egyptian tradition, shares this day's commemoration with his monthly feast. Menas, an Egyptian soldier in the Roman army, confessed Christ before his commanders in Phrygia and was executed, his body thereafter transported back to Egypt where it was buried at a place revealed by miraculous signs. The site became the great shrine of Abu Mena west of Alexandria, one of the most important pilgrimage centers of the ancient world, where countless healings were recorded and the famous Menas ampullae — small flasks of holy oil bearing the image of the saint between two camels — were distributed to pilgrims across the Christian world. His name, possibly deriving from the Egyptian Men, meaning 'established' or 'enduring,' proved prophetic of a memory that has endured through the centuries.

The holy Apostle Thomas, called Didymus — the Twin — completes this day's great assembly. Thomas, whose name in the Aramaic means 'twin,' is beloved in the Ethiopian tradition both for his doubt transformed into the supreme confession ('My Lord and my God!') and for his missionary journey to India, where he is venerated to this day as the founder of the ancient Christian community of the Malabar coast. Thomas carried the Gospel to the far eastern reaches of the known world, as far as the Parthian empire and beyond; his willingness to place his hand in the wounds of the Risen Lord makes him the apostolic witness to the reality of the Incarnation and Resurrection in their bodily truth.

The theological seal of this tenth day of Tahsas is the mystery of witness borne across distance. Matra witnesses in the moment of death; Menas witnesses from the sacred earth of Egypt that receives pilgrims from every nation; Thomas witnesses to the farthest boundaries of the inhabited world. All three together speak of the universal scope of the Incarnation: the Word who became flesh in a particular place and time is Lord of all places and all times, and His witnesses are dispatched to every corner of creation that all flesh might hear and be healed.
